components.name: actors
components.description: Roster of responsibilities owned by named actors.
components.purpose: Use to show 'who owns what' across a process, scoring policy, or org chart. Two-column layout: actor on left, responsibilities on right.

capacity.note: the ledger reads cleanly up to about six rows

rows.description: One row per responsibility. Each li leads with the responsibility label — rendered bold automatically (no `**…**` needed) — then a trailing inline-code actor name (rendered as a right-aligned categorical pill), then an optional nested bullet carrying a one-line body.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: actors -->

## Who owns each part of the process.

- Owns the first part `First actor`
  - One-line note on what that ownership covers.
- Owns the second part `Second actor`
  - One-line note.
- Owns the third part `Third actor`
  - One-line note.

whenToUse.title: Who owns what
whenToUse.body: Each row pairs a named actor with the slice of work they own. Use when the audience needs to know accountability, not process flow.

whenToUse.title: Three to six actors
whenToUse.body: The ledger reads cleanly up to about six rows. Past that, split the roster across two slides or roll up adjacent roles.

whenToUse.title: One-line responsibilities
whenToUse.body: Each actor's body is a short responsibility summary, not a job description. Detail belongs on a follow-up slide or in an appendix.

antiPatterns.title: Process sequence
antiPatterns.body: If the rows describe stages in order, use list-steps or process-flow. actors is for parallel ownership, not handoff sequence.

antiPatterns.title: Long per-actor prose
antiPatterns.body: More than one sentence per row crowds the ledger. Move the detail to a dedicated slide and keep actors as the index.

antiPatterns.title: Roles without names
antiPatterns.body: If the labels are job titles in the abstract ("the engineer"), reach for cards-stack or list. The actors layout earns its weight when the names are named.

related.name: list-tabular
related.when: rows are reference entries, not owners

related.name: cards-stack
related.when: each item needs two sentences of body text

related.name: list
related.when: declared statements — the `principles` variant

related.name: glossary
related.when: the left column is a term, not an actor

components.name: agenda
components.description: Auto-numbered table of contents for the deck.
components.purpose: Use as the second slide of any multi-section deck. Numbers are generated; authors just write the section titles. Five interchangeable styles — the default `ledger` (a contents page with optional page references), plus `circles`, `rail`, `cards`, and `checks` — all compose with the `progress-N` 'you are here' modifier.

capacity.note: past six sections the list crowds

title.description: Slide heading — typically 'Agenda' or 'What we'll cover'.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: agenda -->

## What this deck covers.

1. First section title
2. Second section title
3. Third section title
4. Fourth section title

whenToUse.title: Second slide of the deck
whenToUse.body: Right after the title, before the first section. Orients the audience and sets the cadence of what's coming.

whenToUse.title: Three to six sections
whenToUse.body: Sweet spot is four. Past six sections the list crowds and the audience stops counting. Roll up or split the deck.

whenToUse.title: Reuse with progress variants
whenToUse.body: Drop the same agenda between sections with `progress-N` to show how far through the deck the room is. Lightweight wayfinding.

whenToUse.title: Pick a style, optionally with page refs
whenToUse.body: The default `ledger` reads as a contents page; add `circles`, `rail`, `cards`, or `checks` to change the structure. On `ledger`, end an item with an inline-code page ref — e.g. `` `p.15` `` — to print a right-aligned page number with a leader; omit it for just number + title.

antiPatterns.title: Sub-bullets per section
antiPatterns.body: The agenda is a wayfinder, not a treatment. If a section needs decomposition, that belongs on a section divider when the section opens — not here.

antiPatterns.title: Unnumbered list
antiPatterns.body: Authoring with `-` instead of `1.` loses the numbered chrome the layout depends on. Always use ordered list syntax.

antiPatterns.title: Single-section decks
antiPatterns.body: If the deck has no sections to enumerate, skip the agenda. Empty wayfinding is more friction than no wayfinding.

antiPatterns.title: More than six sections
antiPatterns.body: A single agenda slide holds up to six sections at a legible row height; beyond that the rows crowd the footer. Group related items under fewer headings, or split the agenda across two slides.

related.name: divider
related.when: marking a section boundary without restating the menu

related.name: list
related.when: single-line takeaways — the `takeaway` variant

related.name: title
related.when: the slide immediately preceding the agenda

components.name: authority-chain
components.description: Provenance chain — statute to regulation to guidance to case, walked in order.
components.purpose: Use when the audience needs to see how a rule descends: what the statute says, how the agency implemented it, what guidance interpreted it, and what cases have applied it. Ordered list because the order is the argument.

heading.description: Slide heading naming the rule whose chain is being walked.

tiers.description: Ordered list of authority tiers (Statute, Regulation, Guidance, Case) — not hyperlinks. Each leads with the tier label; nested ul carries the citation (code) and the one-line gloss.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: authority-chain -->

## Rule name — the chain, tier by tier.

1. Statute
   - `Citation reference`
   - One-line gloss naming the body that issued it and what it does.
2. Regulation
   - `Citation reference`
   - One-line gloss naming the agency rule.
3. Guidance
   - `Citation reference`
   - One-line gloss naming the staff guidance.
4. Case
   - `Citation reference`
   - One-line gloss naming the precedent.

whenToUse.title: Provenance is the argument
whenToUse.body: Use when the audience needs to see exactly where a rule comes from and how it has been interpreted. The chain itself is the evidence that the obligation is grounded, not invented.

whenToUse.title: Tier labels carry the read
whenToUse.body: Statute, regulation, guidance, case — each tier has a different legal weight. The left-rail label tells the audience what kind of source they are looking at before they read the citation.

whenToUse.title: Three to five tiers, no more
whenToUse.body: The chain reads top-to-bottom on a single canvas. Past five rows the connectors compress and the tier labels lose room. Group sub-cases into the parent row's gloss or split into two slides.

antiPatterns.title: Flat list of citations
antiPatterns.body: If the rows have no tier hierarchy, use `list-criteria` or `regulatory-update`. authority-chain earns its chrome only when the descent from statute to case is the point.

antiPatterns.title: Missing citation chip
antiPatterns.body: The inline-code citation is the row's anchor; without it the gloss reads as opinion. Always cite, even for guidance and case rows.

antiPatterns.title: Out-of-order tiers
antiPatterns.body: The chain reads as a descent: statute first, case last. Reversing it or skipping a tier breaks the metaphor the audience is using to follow you.

related.name: regulatory-update
related.when: period-bounded changelog rather than a single rule's lineage

related.name: list-criteria
related.when: flat enumeration of requirements without tier hierarchy

related.name: list-steps
related.when: the rows are procedural steps rather than authority tiers

components.name: big-number
components.description: Single oversized number as the focal claim.
components.purpose: Use to make one metric land. The number should be the headline — supporting text is one short caption. The whole slide is the chart.

number.description: First list item: the giant number.

caption.description: One-line caption below the number (nested bullet).

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: big-number -->

`Optional eyebrow`

- 92%
  - of the audience remembers a single number from a deck.

whenToUse.title: One metric carries the slide
whenToUse.body: When the audience needs to remember exactly one number from this part of the deck. The whole slide is the chart — no surrounding context, no comparisons, no axes.

whenToUse.title: Headline that earns the canvas
whenToUse.body: Reach for big-number when the metric is the argument: cost reduced by 4×, audience reach grew 92%, time-to-decision dropped from 14 days to 4. One claim, one canvas.

whenToUse.title: Eyebrow names the metric class
whenToUse.body: The inline-code eyebrow contextualizes the number ("Audience recall", "Q3 revenue", "Latency p99"). The number is the value; the eyebrow is the label.

antiPatterns.title: Multiple metrics on one slide
antiPatterns.body: Two big numbers on one canvas dilute both. Use `stats` for a row of three metrics or `kpi` for a grid of four; reserve big-number for genuinely solo claims.

antiPatterns.title: Caption longer than one line
antiPatterns.body: If the caption needs a sentence to explain, the number isn't carrying the slide. Either trim the number's claim or move to `content` where prose has room.

antiPatterns.title: Decorative numbers without an argument
antiPatterns.body: "99.99% uptime" by itself is a boast, not a claim. Big-number works when the number is the answer to a question the audience came in with.

related.name: stats
related.when: row of 2-3 metrics, comparable visual weight

related.name: kpi
related.when: grid of 4-6 metrics with status indicators

related.name: split-panel
related.when: the metric needs a paragraph of context alongside it

related.name: content
related.when: the argument is mostly prose with a number embedded

components.name: cards-grid
components.description: 2–4 parallel items, similar weight, scannable in a grid.
components.purpose: Use when the audience needs to compare or scan a small set of options at a glance. Avoid for more than 4 items — split into multiple slides. For ordered/numbered steps, use list-steps instead.

capacity.note: the grid loses scannability past four cards

cards.description: Each list item becomes one card. Authoring contract: a top-level bullet is the card title (renders bold by default); an indented bullet underneath carries the body text (renders normal weight via the nested-list rule).

insight.description: Optional key-insight panel above the cards.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: cards-grid -->

## Slide heading.

- First card title
  - Body text for the first card, one sentence.
- Second card title
  - Body text for the second card, one sentence.
- Third card title
  - Body text for the third card, one sentence.
- Fourth card title
  - Body text for the fourth card, one sentence.

whenToUse.title: Parallel items
whenToUse.body: Four cards or fewer, each item gets equal weight in the layout. Audience compares them at a glance.

whenToUse.title: Scannable at a glance
whenToUse.body: The audience absorbs the whole set in one look — no scrolling, no eye-leaping between rows.

whenToUse.title: Equal information density
whenToUse.body: Each card carries roughly the same text length. Uneven density makes the grid feel unbalanced.

whenToUse.title: Order is decorative
whenToUse.body: When sequence carries meaning, use list-steps or list-criteria instead. cards-grid is for parallel options.

antiPatterns.title: More than 4 items
antiPatterns.body: Split into multiple slides instead. The grid loses scannability past 4 cards.

antiPatterns.title: Order carries meaning
antiPatterns.body: Use list-steps or list-criteria. cards-grid is for parallel options, not sequences.

antiPatterns.title: Lopsided density
antiPatterns.body: Equalize the prose when one card has three sentences and the rest have one. Otherwise change layout.

antiPatterns.title: Inline-code-only body
antiPatterns.body: A body bullet containing only `code` gets promoted to an eyebrow label. Mix it with surrounding prose.

related.name: list-steps
related.when: items carry an explicit sequence

related.name: cards-stack
related.when: items stack vertically as full-width rows

related.name: compare-prose
related.when: two-option comparison, side by side

related.name: verdict-grid
related.when: comparing options against shared criteria

components.name: cards-stack
components.description: Parallel items stacked vertically, full-width cards.
components.purpose: Use when the items want vertical reading order — sequential exploration rather than a-glance comparison. 2–4 items work best (a fourth fits with the `compact` modifier).

capacity.note: past four rows the stack overflows

cards.description: Each list item becomes one stacked card. Authoring contract: a top-level bullet is the card title (renders bold by default); an indented bullet underneath carries the body text. An optional trailing inline `code` on the title line renders as a right-anchored pill.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: cards-stack -->

## Slide heading.

- First card title
  - Body text for the first stacked card, two short sentences max.
- Second card title
  - Body text for the second stacked card.
- Third card title
  - Body text for the third stacked card.

whenToUse.title: Vertical reading order
whenToUse.body: The audience scans top-to-bottom, not grid-style. Use when each card builds on the previous one as the eye moves down the slide.

whenToUse.title: Two sentences per card
whenToUse.body: More body than cards-grid can hold without crowding. cards-stack lets each card carry one or two short sentences without losing the layout balance.

whenToUse.title: Two or three items
whenToUse.body: Sweet spot is three. Past that the slide overflows — split across multiple slides or switch to cards-grid with shorter body text per card.

antiPatterns.title: Five or more items
antiPatterns.body: A fourth card fits with the `compact` modifier; past four the stack overflows. For five or more parallel items reach for cards-grid four, or split across slides.

antiPatterns.title: One-line cards
antiPatterns.body: If each card is a single short phrase, the stack reads as a padded list. Drop to `list` (or its `takeaway` variant) and reclaim the vertical space.

antiPatterns.title: Forced sequence
antiPatterns.body: Cards-stack is parallel content read in vertical order, not a numbered sequence. For explicit steps, use list-steps or list-criteria.

related.name: cards-grid
related.when: three or four parallel items in a scannable grid

related.name: compare-prose
related.when: exactly two items, side by side

related.name: list-steps
related.when: items carry an explicit, ordered sequence

components.name: checklist
components.description: Items with state markers — done, partial, todo.
components.purpose: Use for completion reports, readiness audits, or pre-flight checks. State markers [x]/[-]/[ ]/[/] produce status-colored circles carrying a distinct mark — check / dash / ring / slash — so the shape reads independently of color (colour-blind-safe).

capacity.note: past eight items the checklist overflows

items.description: Each item prefixed with a state marker — [x] done, [-] partial, [ ] todo, or [/] out-of-scope (struck through). Plain text follows the marker; an optional trailing inline-code pill floats right as a status tag.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: checklist -->

## Pre-launch readiness.

- [x] First item that is fully done.
- [x] Second item that is fully done.
- [-] Third item that is partially complete with a caveat.
- [ ] Fourth item that is not yet started.

whenToUse.title: Completion reports
whenToUse.body: Use when the audience needs to see what's done, what's in progress, and what's outstanding. The state marks are the load-bearing signal.

whenToUse.title: Readiness audits
whenToUse.body: Pre-launch, pre-release, pre-flight. A short list where the mix of green / amber / red tells the room whether to proceed.

whenToUse.title: Five to eight items
whenToUse.body: Short enough that the audience can take in the state mix at a glance. Past eight, split into two checklists by phase or owner.

antiPatterns.title: All-done lists
antiPatterns.body: If every item is `[x]` the state markers are decoration. Use `list` (or its `takeaway` variant) for celebratory recaps; checklist earns its weight when the mix matters.

antiPatterns.title: Long per-item prose
antiPatterns.body: Each item is one short line. If a row needs a sentence of explanation, the right home is cards-stack or list-tabular.

antiPatterns.title: Custom state markers
antiPatterns.body: Only `[x]`, `[-]`, `[ ]`, and `[/]` (out-of-scope, struck through) map to the mark palette. Authoring `[?]` or `[!]` renders as literal text and breaks the visual contract.

related.name: list
related.when: items have no state — just bullets

related.name: list-tabular
related.when: rows need a label-plus-description structure, not state

related.name: cards-stack
related.when: each item needs two sentences of body

components.name: citation-card
components.description: Single authoritative reference — heading + citation + verbatim quote + plain-English gloss.
components.purpose: Use when one citation IS the slide. The blockquote carries the verbatim language; the trailing list explains what it means and what we must do about it.

heading.description: Slide heading framing what the citation establishes.

citation.description: Inline-code paragraph with the citation reference (e.g. 'Cal. Civ. Code §1798.140(o) · CCPA/CPRA').

quotation.description: Verbatim quote of the cited language.

gloss.description: Optional plain-English interpretation. Use **What we must do** for the actionable item.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: citation-card -->

## Headline framing what this citation establishes.

`Citation reference · short name`

> Verbatim quotation of the cited language.

- Plain-English interpretation of what the language covers.
- **What we must do.**
  - The concrete action this citation argues for.

whenToUse.title: One citation carries the slide
whenToUse.body: When a single statute, contract clause, regulation, or standard is doing the argumentative work. The citation IS the evidence; the slide gives it the room to be read.

whenToUse.title: Verbatim language matters
whenToUse.body: Reach for citation-card when the exact wording is load-bearing — definitions, scope clauses, exception language. The blockquote preserves the language unmodified so the gloss can interpret it.

whenToUse.title: Audience needs the 'so what'
whenToUse.body: The gloss list translates legalese into plain English and names the concrete action. Without it the slide is a quotation; with it the slide is a decision.

antiPatterns.title: Multiple citations on one slide
antiPatterns.body: If you are stacking two or three statutes, use statute-stack instead — citation-card is built for the canvas-weight treatment of a single authority.

antiPatterns.title: Paraphrased 'quote'
antiPatterns.body: If you are rewriting the source language, drop the citation framing and use content or split-panel pullquote. The whole point of citation-card is verbatim language with attribution.

antiPatterns.title: Gloss longer than the quote
antiPatterns.body: When the plain-English explanation runs three paragraphs, the citation is no longer the focus. Trim the gloss to one sentence plus a `What we must do` action, or move to content.

antiPatterns.title: Plain gloss under the pull-quote variant
antiPatterns.body: The `pull-quote` variant hides any gloss line that does not lead with **bold** — it shows only the `**What we must do**` action. A plain 'In plain English …' interpretation line silently vanishes there. Lead gloss lines with a bold label under pull-quote, or keep them on the default variant.

related.name: statute-stack
related.when: two or three citations need to land on one slide

related.name: quote
related.when: the source is a person, not a document

related.name: split-panel
related.when: a quote with three or four implications

related.name: content
related.when: the citation is one input among several in a prose argument

components.name: closing
components.description: Final slide. Dark canvas mirror of title.
components.purpose: Last slide of every deck. Restates the takeaway or call-to-action. Like title, suppresses header/footer/pagination — the dark canvas signals "we're done."

heading.description: Closing line — takeaway, thank-you, or call to action.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: closing -->
<!-- _paginate: false -->
<!-- _header: '' -->
<!-- _footer: '' -->

## Closing takeaway or call to action

`Optional eyebrow`

whenToUse.title: Last slide of every deck
whenToUse.body: Closes the bookend pair with title. Restates the takeaway, the call to action, or the next-steps line. The dark canvas tells the audience visually that the presentation is over.

whenToUse.title: Single takeaway line
whenToUse.body: The h2 is the slide. Keep it to one editorial line that summarizes the whole deck or names the action the audience should take. The eyebrow can carry a sub-line or link.

whenToUse.title: Accent modifier for emphasis
whenToUse.body: Pair with the universal `accent` modifier to recolor the focal heading. Useful when the closing line is a quote or a key decision the deck has been building toward.

antiPatterns.title: Multi-line heading
antiPatterns.body: Keep the closing line to one editorial sentence. The layout is centered and large — two-line closings get cramped and lose impact.

antiPatterns.title: Header or footer overrides
antiPatterns.body: Don't reinstate `_header:` or `_footer:` on the closing. The dark canvas is the "we're done" signal; chrome breaks it. Use the `silent` modifier to suppress all three in one token.

antiPatterns.title: Mid-deck closing
antiPatterns.body: If the audience needs a strong statement mid-deck, use `big-number` or `content` with the `dark` modifier. Reserving closing for the final slide preserves its bookend role.

related.name: title
related.when: opens the deck — same dark-bookend chrome

related.name: divider
related.when: mid-deck section breaks — same dark canvas

related.name: big-number
related.when: single emphatic statement mid-deck without the bookend signal

components.name: code
components.description: Single fenced code block as the slide's centerpiece.
components.purpose: Use when the code IS the slide — an API snippet, a config example, a migration. For comparing two versions, use compare-code.

title.description: Slide heading framing what the code shows.

code.description: Fenced code block — language tag drives syntax highlighting.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: code -->

## What the new endpoint looks like.

```js
app.post('/api/v2/auth', async (req, res) => {
  const session = await issueSession(req.body);
  res.json({ session });
});
```

whenToUse.title: The code is the argument
whenToUse.body: When a single snippet answers the question on the slide — the shape of an API call, the surface of a config, the body of a migration. Authoring follows the snippet, not the other way around.

whenToUse.title: Language hint earns the highlight
whenToUse.body: Always include the language tag on the fence (```js, ```python, ```sql). The highlighter only triggers when the language is named; without it the slide reads as undifferentiated mono.

whenToUse.title: Twenty lines or fewer
whenToUse.body: Past about twenty lines the type shrinks below boardroom legibility. Trim ruthlessly — keep imports out, elide bodies with `// ...`, and let the rest of the deck carry the surrounding context.

antiPatterns.title: Comparing two versions
antiPatterns.body: If you need before/after, use compare-code — it gives both snippets parallel framing. code is for a single snippet doing one job.

antiPatterns.title: Code-as-decoration
antiPatterns.body: A screenshot of an IDE or a snippet the audience cannot read defeats the layout. If the code is too long to legibly fit, the slide isn't a code slide — it's a content slide that talks about code.

antiPatterns.title: No language hint
antiPatterns.body: A bare fence renders as undifferentiated mono. Always tag the language so the highlighter and the reviewer both know what they are looking at.

related.name: compare-code
related.when: before/after snippet comparison

related.name: diagram
related.when: the architecture matters more than the code

related.name: math
related.when: the equation is the argument, not the implementation

related.name: content
related.when: code is one piece of a longer prose explanation

components.name: compare-code
components.description: Two fenced code blocks side-by-side, each with a label.
components.purpose: Use to contrast a before/after refactor, two API styles, or two configurations. Each side gets an h3 label and one fenced block.
components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: compare-code -->

## Heading framing the comparison.

### Before

```js
function before() {
  return 'old';
}
```

### After

```js
function after() {
  return 'new';
}
```

whenToUse.title: Concrete code on both sides
whenToUse.body: Both sides hold short, readable snippets — refactor before/after, two API styles, two configurations. The diff is the point of the slide.

whenToUse.title: Equal-length snippets
whenToUse.body: Snippets render side-by-side. Wildly different lengths break the visual balance — trim aggressively or split into two slides.

whenToUse.title: Names the change
whenToUse.body: The h3 label on each side names what the reader is looking at (Before, After, v1, v2). Without labels the audience has to infer.

antiPatterns.title: One side is prose
antiPatterns.body: If one column is code and the other is description, use a single fenced block with surrounding prose. compare-code is for code-versus-code.

antiPatterns.title: Snippets longer than 14 lines
antiPatterns.body: The text shrinks below readability past 14 lines per side. Split into two slides or extract the key delta into a smaller diff.

antiPatterns.title: Three-way comparison
antiPatterns.body: compare-code is binary. For three configurations or three implementations, use prose with successive fenced blocks or a `compare-table`.

related.name: compare-prose
related.when: the change is state, not code

related.name: compare-prose
related.when: the comparison is prose-versus-prose

related.name: redline
related.when: the change is in verbatim text or legal language

related.name: compare-table
related.when: three or more variants on shared dimensions

components.name: compare-prose
components.description: Two prose options side-by-side with a labeled corner tag on each.
components.purpose: Use to weigh two approaches against each other in body text. Add the `chosen` or `decision` modifier to mark the verdict; add `vertical` to stack top/bottom instead of side-by-side.

options.description: Exactly two list items, each one option. The lead text is the option label — it renders bold automatically (no `**…**` needed); follow it with a nested bullet carrying 1–3 sentences.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: compare-prose -->

## Heading framing the comparison.

- First option
  - Two-sentence description of the first option, including the strongest argument for it.
- Second option
  - Two-sentence description of the second option, including the strongest argument for it.

whenToUse.title: Two prose alternatives
whenToUse.body: Both sides are full sentences of argument, not lists of facts. The audience reads each column as a paragraph and weighs them against each other.

whenToUse.title: Equal-density prose
whenToUse.body: Each card carries roughly the same body length. One short and one long breaks the visual symmetry that makes the comparison legible.

whenToUse.title: Add a verdict modifier when chosen
whenToUse.body: Layer `chosen`, `decision`, or `vertical` to name the editorial intent. The default (neutral two-up) reads as still-being-decided.

antiPatterns.title: Code comparison
antiPatterns.body: Use `compare-code` for two fenced blocks. compare-prose is for sentences, not snippets.

antiPatterns.title: Three or more options
antiPatterns.body: compare-prose is strictly two. For three or more, use `cards-grid three` or `verdict-grid` with criteria badges.

antiPatterns.title: Verbatim text differences
antiPatterns.body: When the diff lives inside the prose itself — legal language, contract clauses — use `redline` so insertions and deletions render inline.

related.name: compare-code
related.when: the columns are code, not prose

related.name: split-compare
related.when: the verdict needs a bottom recommendation bar

related.name: verdict-grid
related.when: three or more options scored against shared criteria

related.name: decision
related.when: the verdict slide that lands after a comparison

components.name: compare-table
components.description: Multi-row comparison table with consistent columns.
components.purpose: Use when you have 3+ options or 4+ rows of criteria. Wider data than compare-prose can hold legibly.

capacity.note: the table density crowds past six rows

table.description: Markdown table with header row and 2+ data rows.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: compare-table -->

## Heading framing the comparison.

| Criterion | Option A | Option B | Option C |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| First criterion | Value | Value | Value |
| Second criterion | Value | Value | Value |
| Third criterion | Value | Value | Value |

whenToUse.title: Wider than compare-prose
whenToUse.body: Three or more options, or four or more rows of criteria. compare-prose maxes out at two columns and short bodies; compare-table scales further.

whenToUse.title: Cells are short phrases
whenToUse.body: Each cell is a value, a phrase, or a state marker — not a paragraph. If the cells need sentences, use `verdict-grid` or `cards-stack`.

whenToUse.title: Stable column meaning
whenToUse.body: Every row reads the same way across columns. Mixing column meanings row-to-row breaks the table's scannability.

antiPatterns.title: Cells full of prose
antiPatterns.body: Long sentences in a table cell wrap awkwardly and force the column wider. Move to `verdict-grid` for criteria with body text, or `cards-stack` for full prose rows.

antiPatterns.title: More than 6 rows
antiPatterns.body: Past 6 rows the table density crowds the slide. Split into two slides or summarise the rows that don't differentiate.

antiPatterns.title: State-marker rows
antiPatterns.body: When most cells are pass/fail/partial badges, the right layout is `obligation-matrix` or `verdict-grid`. compare-table is for textual values.

related.name: compare-prose
related.when: exactly two options with prose bodies

related.name: verdict-grid
related.when: options scored against criteria with pass/partial/fail badges

related.name: obligation-matrix
related.when: many regimes compared against shared obligations

related.name: cards-stack
related.when: each row needs full-prose breathing room rather than a tabular cell

components.name: content
components.description: Generic prose slide — heading plus paragraphs or a short list.
components.purpose: The catch-all for explanatory content that doesn't fit a more structured layout. Resist using it when a more specific component (cards-grid, stats, compare-prose) would shape the content better.

body.description: Paragraphs or a short bullet list under the heading. Keep under ~40 words.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: content -->

## Slide heading.

The explanatory paragraph that develops the heading goes here. Keep the slide under forty words.

- Optional supporting point one.
- Optional supporting point two.

whenToUse.title: Explanatory prose that doesn't shape
whenToUse.body: A paragraph that develops one idea. No comparisons to spell out, no inventory to grid, no metric to highlight — just prose with a heading. The catch-all when shape would be forced.

whenToUse.title: Under forty words
whenToUse.body: Content slides earn their place when they're brief. Past 40 words the slide becomes a wall of text and the audience stops reading. Trim or split into two slides.

whenToUse.title: Optional short bullet list
whenToUse.body: If the paragraph wants two or three loose qualifications, a bullet list below the prose is fine. For more than that, the content is really structured — move to a `list` or `cards-stack` slide.

antiPatterns.title: Forced shape into prose
antiPatterns.body: If the content is a comparison, use compare-prose. If it's a list of options, use cards-grid. If it's a sequence, use list-steps. Reaching for content when shape exists wastes the slide.

antiPatterns.title: Wall of text
antiPatterns.body: More than 40 words and the audience tunes out. The layout doesn't fight back — it'll happily render a 200-word paragraph that nobody reads. Split or trim.

antiPatterns.title: Multiple headings
antiPatterns.body: Content carries one heading and one idea. Two h2s on one slide reads as two slides crammed together. Split into two content slides or use a structured layout.

related.name: quote
related.when: the prose IS a quote — let the quotation chrome carry it

related.name: big-number
related.when: the prose IS a metric — let the number carry it

related.name: cards-grid
related.when: the prose IS a parallel list of items

related.name: compare-prose
related.when: the prose IS a two-way comparison

related.name: list-steps
related.when: the prose IS an ordered sequence

components.name: decision
components.description: The verdict slide — one chosen path, named explicitly.
components.purpose: Use after a comparison slide to land the decision. The justifications render as one unified categorical strip — co-equal cards that together signal a single resolved verdict; the heading carries the decision, not a focal/subordinated split.

options.description: List items. Authoring contract: a top-level bullet is the option name (renders bold by default); an indented bullet underneath carries the short rationale. The cards render as a unified strip of co-equal categorical tags; the verdict is carried by the heading, not by emphasizing one card.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: decision -->

## What we are doing.

- Chosen path
  - One-line rationale for the decision.
- Rejected option
  - One-line rationale for why this didn't fit.

whenToUse.title: Land the verdict
whenToUse.body: Follows a comparison slide to make the chosen path unambiguous. The heading carries the verb of the decision; the cards substantiate it.

whenToUse.title: Two to four justifications
whenToUse.body: Each card is one short rationale — the chosen path first, then a Why-not card for each rejected alternative. More than four crowds the horizontal strip.

whenToUse.title: After the comparison
whenToUse.body: Pair with `compare-prose` or `split-compare` upstream — that slide does the weighing, this slide lands the answer. Reaching for decision without a prior comparison reads as edict.

antiPatterns.title: No clear chosen path
antiPatterns.body: If the cards don't resolve to a single verdict, the slide is back to being a comparison. Use `compare-prose` or `split-compare`; reserve decision for the resolved call.

antiPatterns.title: Long body per card
antiPatterns.body: Each card is one sentence of rationale. Paragraphs belong on the comparison slide upstream, not on the verdict slide.

antiPatterns.title: Generic heading
antiPatterns.body: The h2 carries the decision verb — Build, not buy. Adopt the framework. Pause the rollout. A heading like Next steps wastes the focal real estate.

related.name: compare-prose
related.when: the comparison slide that should precede decision

related.name: split-compare
related.when: comparison and verdict on one slide instead of two

related.name: closing
related.when: the deck ends without a single-call verdict

related.name: big-number
related.when: the decision is a quantitative commitment

components.name: diagram
components.description: Mermaid diagram as the slide's centerpiece.
components.purpose: Use for relational or topological visuals — flowcharts, sequence diagrams, state machines, ER diagrams. The diagram should occupy at least half the slide.

title.description: Slide heading framing what the diagram shows.

mermaid.description: Fenced ```mermaid block, pre-rendered to SVG at build time.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: diagram -->

## How signals move from input to decision.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A[Input] --> B[Process]
  B --> C{Decision}
  C -->|yes| D[Path A]
  C -->|no| E[Path B]
```

whenToUse.title: Relational structure is the message
whenToUse.body: Flowcharts, sequence diagrams, state machines, ER diagrams, journey maps. The relationships between nodes carry meaning the audience needs to see at a glance.

whenToUse.title: Diagram occupies at least half the canvas
whenToUse.body: If the heading and prose dominate and the diagram is a sidebar, it is a content slide that happens to have a diagram. Reach for diagram when the graph IS the slide.

whenToUse.title: Palette tokens render automatically
whenToUse.body: Mermaid blocks are pre-rendered to SVG with palette tokens injected via `%%{init}%%`. Don't hand-set colors — the diagram inherits the active theme so dark / accent variants work without re-authoring.

antiPatterns.title: Tabular data on axes
antiPatterns.body: Quantitative datapoints across two axes are not flowchart material. Use quadrant, radar, progress, piechart, or timeline-list — the series-substance components are designed for plotted data.

antiPatterns.title: Twenty-node spaghetti
antiPatterns.body: Past a dozen nodes the diagram stops being scannable. Split into two slides, hide leaf nodes behind a summary node, or move to a multi-page diagram-doc reference.

antiPatterns.title: Inline color overrides
antiPatterns.body: Hand-set node colors break the theme contract. Let palette tokens drive everything; if you need to highlight one node, use mermaid's `class` mechanism so the highlight survives theme remapping.

related.name: code
related.when: the implementation, not the topology, is the argument

related.name: quadrant
related.when: items positioned by two numeric attributes

related.name: radar
related.when: options rated across several criteria

related.name: timeline-list
related.when: the graph is a sequence in time, not a topology

related.name: content
related.when: the diagram is one element in a prose slide

components.name: divider
components.description: Section boundary slide. Dark canvas with a single heading.
components.purpose: Marks the start of a major section. Use sparingly — every divider is a context switch for the audience. A 30-slide deck typically has 3-5 dividers; more becomes navigation noise.

eyebrow.description: Optional section number or category label above the heading.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: divider -->
<!-- _paginate: false -->
<!-- _header: '' -->
<!-- _footer: '' -->

`Section 01`

## Section name

whenToUse.title: Major section starts
whenToUse.body: Marks the boundary between two themed sections of the deck. The dark canvas is a strong context-switch signal — use it when the audience needs to re-orient.

whenToUse.title: Sparingly
whenToUse.body: A 30-slide deck typically has 3-5 dividers. More becomes navigation noise; the signal weakens if every third slide is a divider.

whenToUse.title: With an eyebrow
whenToUse.body: An inline-code paragraph above the heading stamps a section number or category label. Useful for serialized decks where the audience needs to remember which section they're in.

whenToUse.title: Light variant for sub-section orientation
whenToUse.body: The `light` variant keeps the bright body-slide canvas and centers the heading at h2 weight — a lighter context switch for narrowing focus within a section, between a dark divider and the content slides. (Absorbed the standalone `subtopic` component on 2026-06-07.)

antiPatterns.title: More than five per deck
antiPatterns.body: Each divider is a hard context switch. Too many dilutes the signal and slows the audience. Group related content under fewer sections instead.

antiPatterns.title: Section title that doesn't earn a section
antiPatterns.body: If the next 3-4 slides aren't a coherent unit, the `light` variant (bright canvas, centered) is the right tool. Reserve the dark divider for genuine section starts.

antiPatterns.title: Header or footer overrides
antiPatterns.body: Don't reinstate `_header:` or `_footer:` on a divider. The dark canvas is meant to be uninterrupted; chrome belongs on body slides.

related.name: title
related.when: opens the deck — same dark-bookend chrome

related.name: closing
related.when: closes the deck — completes the bookend trio

components.name: funnel
components.description: Tapering stages that show where a flow drops off, with the conversion rate between each.
components.purpose: Use for a pipeline that narrows — a sales / conversion funnel, a hiring or grant pipeline, an onboarding flow. Each stage's band width is proportional to its value; the stage-to-stage conversion % is printed in the gaps so the leak is the read.

title.description: Slide heading — name the flow and, ideally, the takeaway (‘Where the pipeline leaks’).

stages.description: One li per stage, in flow order (widest first). Lead with the stage label, then a trailing inline-code value — `Signups \`4,800\``. Commas and units are tolerated; the largest value sets full width. Three to seven stages read best.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: funnel -->

## Where the flow drops off.

- First stage `1000`
- Second stage `600`
- Third stage `320`
- Fourth stage `140`

whenToUse.title: The story is the drop-off
whenToUse.body: A funnel earns its shape when each stage is a strict subset of the one above and the audience needs to see WHERE the flow narrows — the leaky step, not just the totals. If the stages aren't a shrinking pipeline, use `progress` or `stats`.

whenToUse.title: Three to seven stages
whenToUse.body: Two stages is just a ratio (use `big-number` or `stats`); past seven the bands thin out and the conversion labels crowd. Most pipelines fit in four to six.

whenToUse.title: Values in flow order, widest first
whenToUse.body: Author the stages top-to-bottom as the flow runs, largest count first. The widest value sets full width and every band scales to it; the conversion % is computed from each value to the next.

antiPatterns.title: Stages that aren't a subset
antiPatterns.body: If a later stage can exceed an earlier one (it's a category breakdown, not a pipeline), the taper lies. Use `piechart` for parts of a whole or `progress` for independent metrics.

antiPatterns.title: A funnel of two stages
antiPatterns.body: Two bands is a single conversion rate dressed up as a chart. State it as a `big-number` (‘18% convert’) or a two-tile `stats` instead.

antiPatterns.title: Non-monotonic values
antiPatterns.body: Values that rise and fall make the trapezoids bulge and the conversion %s read oddly. A funnel assumes a monotonic narrowing; for an up-and-down series use a `progress` or a chart with an axis.

related.name: progress
related.when: independent metrics as labelled bars, not a narrowing pipeline

related.name: stats
related.when: a row of headline figures with no drop-off relationship

related.name: piechart
related.when: parts of a single whole rather than sequential stages

related.name: list-steps
related.when: the stages are a process to walk through, not values to compare

related.name: big-number
related.when: a single conversion rate is the whole story

components.name: gantt
components.description: Gantt chart — task bars across a date axis.
components.purpose: Use for project plans with overlapping or staggered tasks. Each task is a bar on the time axis; bars can span multiple periods and carry status tints.

tasks.description: Outer li per workstream lane; nested bullets per task. Each task carries inline-code tokens for span (`Q1 → Q2`, or an en-dash / -> delimiter) and an optional status pill; the two pills may appear in any order. Status vocabulary: on-track / done / live / at-risk / warn / blocked / fail / deferred / pilot / decision. The range axis recognises quarters (Q1–Q4) or months (Jan–Dec); other vocabularies fall back to a four-column axis with no ticks.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: gantt -->

`2026 Q1 → 2026 Q4`

## What ships in each phase, by workstream.

- First workstream
  - First task `Q1 → Q2` `done`
  - Second task `Q2 → Q3` `live`
  - Third task `Q3 → Q4` `at-risk`
- Second workstream
  - First task `Q1 → Q2` `done`
  - Second task `Q2 → Q3`

whenToUse.title: Overlapping work across lanes
whenToUse.body: When tasks run in parallel across multiple workstreams and the audience needs to see who is busy when. The lane-stacked bars make concurrency visible at a glance.

whenToUse.title: Span is the story
whenToUse.body: Each bar's length encodes its duration. Use gantt when start dates, end dates, and overlap are what you want the audience to remember.

whenToUse.title: Status pills add a second channel
whenToUse.body: Tint bars with `done` / `live` / `at-risk` / `blocked` to layer health onto schedule. The plan reads as both 'when' and 'how it's going' in one chart. Since the bars carry no status text, a swatch+label status key is emitted automatically below the chart for the statuses present.

antiPatterns.title: Single workstream
antiPatterns.body: One lane of bars is a timeline, not a gantt. Use `timeline` or `list-steps` when there is no parallel work to coordinate.

antiPatterns.title: More than five lanes
antiPatterns.body: Past five workstreams the bars compress and the labels crowd. Group lanes (collapse 'SDK' subdomains into 'SDK') or split into two slides.

antiPatterns.title: No spans
antiPatterns.body: If tasks are point-in-time milestones rather than durations, use `timeline` or `roadmap milestones`. gantt earns its chrome only when bars have meaningful length.

related.name: roadmap
related.when: phased grid of deliverables across workstreams without continuous spans

related.name: kanban
related.when: current state by stage rather than schedule by lane

related.name: list-steps
related.when: sequential process with descriptive steps, no parallel lanes

components.name: glossary
components.description: Two-column term/definition table with auto-derived alphabetic range pill.
components.purpose: Use for jargon-heavy decks where the audience needs a reference page. The runtime auto-adds a range pill (e.g. 'A – G') to the heading.

entries.description: Nested bullets: outer li is the term, inner li is its one-line definition. A runtime transform converts the list into a two-column table and derives the alphabetic range pill from the first and last terms, so terms should be authored in alphabetical order; without the Lattice runtime the raw nested list renders unstyled.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: glossary -->

## Glossary

- Adjacency
  - The relationship between two slides that share an audience or context.
- Anchor
  - A title, divider, or closing slide that orients the audience.
- Cadence
  - The deck's pacing — how much new information per slide.

whenToUse.title: Jargon-heavy decks
whenToUse.body: When the audience needs a reference page they can flip back to. Acronyms, domain terms, internal names — anything the speaker won't define inline.

whenToUse.title: Five to eight entries per slide
whenToUse.body: The ledger is sized for a short page. For longer glossaries, split alphabetically across multiple slides — the runtime stamps each with its range pill.

whenToUse.title: Short definitions
whenToUse.body: Each definition is one sentence — a working gloss, not an essay. Long definitions belong on their own divider (light variant) slide where the term is the heading.

antiPatterns.title: Multi-sentence definitions
antiPatterns.body: Each entry is one short line. If a definition needs context or examples, the term deserves its own slide — use a divider (light variant) with the term as the heading.

antiPatterns.title: Mixed term lengths
antiPatterns.body: If some terms are single words and others are full phrases, the left column gets ragged. Trim long terms to their canonical short form.

antiPatterns.title: Hand-written range pill
antiPatterns.body: The runtime derives the range pill (e.g. "A – G") from the entries. Authoring it into the heading double-stamps it.

related.name: list-tabular
related.when: rows are key/value reference, not term/definition

related.name: divider
related.when: lighter mid-section orientation — the bright-canvas `light` variant

related.name: actors
related.when: the left column is a named person, not a term

related.name: list
related.when: declared statements — the `principles` variant

components.name: image
components.description: Image as the slide's anchor, with optional text alongside — composition adapts to the asset and the deck.
components.purpose: Use when a visual carries meaning on its own. You hand it any rectangle; the layout reads the asset's aspect at build time and, with the deck orientation, RESOLVES the composition for you — no modifier needed. The default is `clean` (a floated card shaped to the photo, ≈ zero crop); extreme aspects auto-pick `split` (shown whole) or `spotlight` (full-bleed cover). Name a composition to override: `clean` · `split` · `spotlight` · `gallery` (contain-on-matte, for diagrams) · `statement` (full-bleed + scrim + title). `mirror` flips the image side. Legacy `full`/`contain`/`museum` still work (→ spotlight/gallery/gallery).

image.description: Marp background image syntax: `![bg](path)` or `![bg right](path)` — rendered as a CSS background-image on the `.lattice-bg` panel (no ``).

heading.description: Optional heading in the text slot.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: image -->

## Text leads; the image earns its place.

Swap the bg image below for your own asset — any aspect. The layout reads its shape and resolves the composition for you (a floated card, a full-height column, a full-bleed cover). Name a composition (`image spotlight`, `image gallery`, …) only to override.

![bg](sample-photo-wide.svg)

whenToUse.title: The visual carries meaning
whenToUse.body: Product screenshots, architectural photographs, plots, satellite imagery — anywhere the image makes the argument and the prose is annotation. If the visual is decorative, drop it and use `content` instead.

whenToUse.title: Let the layout resolve it — override only with intent
whenToUse.body: Drop in any aspect and the layout reads it: a moderate photo gets the `clean` floated card, a tall/wide one gets `split` (shown whole), a canvas-matching one gets `spotlight` (full-bleed). Override only when you mean it: `image gallery` to contain a diagram zero-crop, `image statement` for a scrim-and-title hero, `image spotlight` to force a full-bleed cover (accepting the crop).

whenToUse.title: Caption earns its line
whenToUse.body: If the prose alongside the image just describes what the image shows, drop it — the audience can see the picture. The text slot is for the so-what: what the audience should take away from the visual.

antiPatterns.title: Decorative stock photo
antiPatterns.body: A generic photograph of 'people in a meeting' next to a content slide is filler. Use `content` and trust the prose; reserve image for visuals that argue for themselves.

antiPatterns.title: Image too small to read
antiPatterns.body: A diagram or screenshot small enough to fit inside a half-canvas text slot is unreadable from the back of the room. Reach for `image gallery` (contains it whole) or `image spotlight`, or move the diagram to its own `diagram` slide.

antiPatterns.title: Image with five paragraphs of caption
antiPatterns.body: If the prose dominates and the image is a sidebar, you have a `content` slide that happens to have a photo. Either trust the image (drop the prose) or trust the prose (drop the image).

related.name: diagram
related.when: the visual is a Mermaid graph, not a photo or screenshot

related.name: content
related.when: the slide is mostly prose with one inline visual

related.name: title
related.when: the image is a bookend hero, not the body of a slide

related.name: quote
related.when: the slide is a quotation, not an image

components.name: journey
components.description: Native user-journey chart — sections of tasks, each tagged with actor(s) and a 1-5 mood. Renders as section bars, task chips, plumb lines, and mood faces.
components.purpose: Use when a process or experience needs charting as a horizontal sequence of moments, each scored for affect. Five variants reshape the same source list: default (Mermaid-style classic), heatmap (mood-tinted chips), curve (mood polyline with axis), swimlane (per-actor rows), weighted (chip widths proportional to `+volume`).

heading.description: Slide heading naming the journey or process.

sections.description: Top-level li per section. Lead with the section name; nested ul carries tasks. Each task carries inline-code tokens: `@actor` (one or more), `:N` mood 1-5, optional `+N` volume (used by .weighted).

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: journey -->

## Walking through my Tuesday morning.

- Wake up
  - Hit snooze `@me` `:2`
  - Make coffee `@me` `:4`
- Commute
  - Subway `@me` `:1`
  - Walk `@me` `:5`
- Work
  - Standup `@team` `:3`
  - Deep work `@me` `:5`

whenToUse.title: Affect is part of the story
whenToUse.body: When a process matters not just for its steps but for how each step feels. The 1-5 mood score makes the emotional contour part of the chart instead of buried in narration.

whenToUse.title: Actors share the trail
whenToUse.body: Use when multiple actors hand off through the sequence — customer, sales, onboarding, support. The `@actor` tokens make the handoff visible on every task chip.

whenToUse.title: One source, five lenses
whenToUse.body: Author the journey once and re-render under any variant. Heatmap for fastest scan, curve for trend, swimlane for actor load, weighted for traffic-mix — same data, different argument.

antiPatterns.title: Process without affect
antiPatterns.body: If the mood scores are all the same or arbitrary, the chart is doing less work than `timeline` or `list-steps`. Reserve journey for sequences where the affect changes meaningfully.

antiPatterns.title: More than ten tasks
antiPatterns.body: Past ten tasks the chips compress and the labels become unreadable. Group into fewer sections, or split the journey at a natural break.

antiPatterns.title: Volume tokens without weighted
antiPatterns.body: The `+N` volume token is meaningful only under the `weighted` variant. On the other four it is parsed but invisible — strip it from the markdown or commit to weighted.

related.name: list-steps
related.when: process needs descriptive body per step, no chart

related.name: gantt
related.when: schedule of overlapping tasks across lanes

related.name: kanban
related.when: current status by stage rather than sequence over time

components.name: kanban
components.description: Kanban board — columns of cards by stage.
components.purpose: Use for status snapshots: what's in each lane (todo/doing/done or similar). Each column is a stage; each card is a work item.

capacity.note: past five lanes the cards compress

lanes.description: Three levels. Outer li = column header as plain text (e.g. Backlog). Each inner li = a card: title then a trailing inline-code size badge (S/M/L/XL; other codes are left in the title). Each card may carry its own nested bullet = a categorical lane label, optionally with a trailing status pill, e.g. - platform `at-risk`. A column titled Done / Completed / Shipped / Closed dims its cards. Status vocabulary matches the shared chart set (on-track / done / live / at-risk / warn / blocked / fail / deferred / pilot / decision).

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: kanban -->

`Eyebrow · context`

## Board status today.

- Backlog
  - First card `S`
    - team-a
  - Second card `M`
    - team-b `at-risk`
- In progress
  - Third card `M`
    - team-a
- Done
  - Fourth card `S`
    - team-b

whenToUse.title: Status snapshot by stage
whenToUse.body: When the audience needs to see what is in each lane right now — backlog, in progress, review, done. The board reads as the current state of the work, not its history or schedule.

whenToUse.title: Mixed card density is informative
whenToUse.body: Lanes that bulge or thin out tell the story — a fat 'in progress' column flags a WIP overload; an empty 'review' column flags a handoff stall. The visual imbalance is the signal.

whenToUse.title: Cards carry size and status meta
whenToUse.body: Trailing inline-code badges (`S`/`M`/`L`/`XL`) sit in the title row; status pills (`at-risk`, `blocked`) push right on the meta row. The card stays scannable while the second channel of information rides along.

antiPatterns.title: Schedule, not status
antiPatterns.body: If the question is when each task ships rather than where it sits today, reach for `gantt` (spans) or `roadmap` (phases). Kanban is a snapshot, not a timeline.

antiPatterns.title: More than five lanes
antiPatterns.body: Past five columns the cards compress and the column headers crowd. Group adjacent stages or split into two boards (e.g. by team) instead.

antiPatterns.title: Cards without meta
antiPatterns.body: A board of bare titles wastes the layout's affordances. Add at least a size badge and a lane label so the audience can scan workload and ownership at a glance.

related.name: gantt
related.when: schedule of overlapping tasks across lanes, not current state

related.name: roadmap
related.when: phased grid of deliverables across workstreams

related.name: checklist
related.when: single list of items with done/in-flight/planned states

related.name: verdict-grid
related.when: options scored against shared criteria, not stage-tracked

components.name: kpi
components.description: Executive KPI system — one base, five layout modifiers.
components.purpose: Use for KPI dashboards with status framing — current value, target, trend, attention-needed. Bare `kpi` resolves to the briefing layout; the five modifiers tune the visual emphasis for different audiences (ops, compliance, investor, headline).

title.description: Slide heading naming the KPI group.

eyebrow.description: Optional inline-code eyebrow above the heading — mono, tracked uppercase (e.g. `Financial · Q4 2026`). Authored as an inline-code paragraph, not a heading, so it stays lint-safe (no heading-order violation).

kpis.description: One li per KPI, authored as an ordered list (`1.`). The lead is the metric value (the big number) — it renders in display type automatically (no `**…**` needed); follow it with nested bullets for the metric name, target/trend, and status pills. A bare value with no nested bullets won't render as the number.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: kpi -->

`Financial · Q4 2026`

## Revenue ahead of plan; margin and cash both expanded.

1. $2.4B
   - Total revenue
   - target $2.2B · +9% `On plan` `Board`
2. 42%
   - Gross margin
   - +2pp QoQ `On plan` `Audit`
3. $1.1B
   - Cash & equivalents
   - +$180M QoQ `On plan` `Investor`

whenToUse.title: Status framing matters as much as the number
whenToUse.body: Reach for kpi when the audience needs value, target, trend, AND status indicator together. For ungoverned metric rows use stats; for a single hero number use big-number.

whenToUse.title: Pick the modifier from the audience
whenToUse.body: Board / investor reviews use the bare briefing default. SRE / SLO reviews use `ops`. Auditor / regulator packs use `compliance`. Year-over-year growth stories use `trajectory`. A single hero metric with body copy uses `spotlight`.

whenToUse.title: One contract across all five
whenToUse.body: Every modifier reads the same `` `eyebrow` `` / `## headline` / `1. value` / nested bullets / status pills authoring contract. Switching modifiers should never require rewriting the prose.

antiPatterns.title: Decorative pills without status semantics
antiPatterns.body: The pills read as status, not freeform tags. Status colour is assigned by each KPI's row position within the modifier — the engine never reads the pill text — so reserve them for the status vocabulary the position implies (`On plan`, `At risk`, `Breaching`, `Compliant`, `Remediating`). Arbitrary labels land a colour that has nothing to do with the words.

antiPatterns.title: More than four KPIs in attention or spotlight
antiPatterns.body: `attention` highlights the metric that needs the room; `spotlight` monumentalises one number. Past four KPIs the visual hierarchy collapses — split into two slides.

antiPatterns.title: No targets, no trends
antiPatterns.body: If the KPIs carry only current values, the slide is a stats row, not a kpi dashboard. Use stats and reclaim the room.

related.name: stats
related.when: metric row without targets or status pills

related.name: big-number
related.when: a single number is the whole argument

related.name: split-panel
related.when: one KPI with a paragraph of supporting prose

related.name: progress
related.when: completion percentages across parallel workstreams

related.name: timeline-list
related.when: milestones in time, not metrics at a moment

components.name: list
components.description: Bulleted list under a heading — plain pills, hairline takeaways, or display-weight principles.
components.purpose: Use when the items are genuinely a flat list of one-line points. The default renders accent-bordered pills; the `takeaway` variant renders hairline-ruled single-line takeaways (former tldr); the `principles` variant renders display-weight numbered statements with a large counter (former principles). For richer per-item structure, prefer cards-grid, cards-stack, or list-tabular.

items.description: List items. Keep each under ~12 words.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: list -->

## Slide heading.

- First short bullet point.
- Second short bullet point.
- Third short bullet point.
- Fourth short bullet point.
- Fifth short bullet point.

whenToUse.title: Genuinely a list
whenToUse.body: Five to six short points, each under twelve words. No internal structure per item — just a heading and the bullets.

whenToUse.title: Numbered when order matters
whenToUse.body: Use `ol` (`1.` source) when sequence is load-bearing; `ul` when order is interchangeable. Numbers render as a tabular leading column.

whenToUse.title: Pills via inline code
whenToUse.body: Inline code at the end of a row becomes a pill (status tag, metric, owner). Lets the list double as a lightweight ledger without changing layout.

whenToUse.title: Takeaways at a section close
whenToUse.body: The `takeaway` variant renders each item as a hairline-ruled single line at message weight — the deck or section's headline points. Add `numbered` for a large accent counter. (Absorbed the standalone `tldr` component on 2026-06-07.)

whenToUse.title: Declared principles or tenets
whenToUse.body: The `principles` variant renders an ordered list of single-sentence declarations at display weight with a large accent counter. Compose `lettered`, `roman`, or `bullet` to switch the counter format. (Absorbed the standalone `principles` component on 2026-06-07.)

antiPatterns.title: Title plus body per item
antiPatterns.body: If each bullet is `**Title.** body`, the layout under-serves it. Move to cards-stack (2-3 items) or list-tabular (5+ rows) instead.

antiPatterns.title: Wall of long bullets
antiPatterns.body: Past twelve words per line the slide becomes paragraph soup. Either trim or move to content for prose, cards-stack for structured items.

antiPatterns.title: Two-item lists
antiPatterns.body: Two bullets read as a thin slide. For pairs, reach for compare-prose — it gives the pair the weight it deserves.

related.name: cards-stack
related.when: each item has a title plus body sentence

related.name: list-tabular
related.when: five or more rows with label-plus-description

related.name: checklist
related.when: items carry state markers (done / partial / todo)

components.name: list-criteria
components.description: Numbered criteria list — each requirement is a row with rationale.
components.purpose: Use to enumerate the criteria a decision must meet, in priority order. Numbering signals weight; each row reads as a complete requirement.

criteria.description: One li per criterion. The lead text is the criterion title — it renders bold automatically (no `**…**` needed); follow it with a nested `- rationale` bullet.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: list-criteria -->

## What every decision must satisfy.

1. First criterion
   - Short rationale for why this matters.
2. Second criterion
   - Short rationale.
3. Third criterion
   - Short rationale.
4. Fourth criterion
   - Short rationale.

whenToUse.title: Criteria that must all be satisfied
whenToUse.body: When the audience needs to read each requirement as a complete gate, not a suggestion. The numbered ledger format signals 'these are the rules' rather than 'here are some options'.

whenToUse.title: Order encodes priority
whenToUse.body: The leading-zero counter (`01`, `02`, …) reads as rank. Put the load-bearing criterion first; the audience uses position as a weight.

whenToUse.title: Three to six rows
whenToUse.body: Below three the ledger feels under-furnished; above six the row gap closes and the audience loses scannability. Group adjacent criteria or split into two slides.

antiPatterns.title: Parallel options, not gates
antiPatterns.body: If the items are alternatives the audience is choosing between, use `cards-grid` or `verdict-grid`. list-criteria is for requirements all of which must hold.

antiPatterns.title: Rationale longer than two lines
antiPatterns.body: Each row is a one-sentence rationale. If a criterion needs a paragraph, lift it to `list-steps` or `split-panel` where the body has room to breathe.

antiPatterns.title: Missing criterion title
antiPatterns.body: The lead line on each li — rendered bold automatically — is what makes the ledger scannable. A naked sentence per row reads as paragraph soup; the title is the structure.

related.name: list-steps
related.when: rows are procedural steps with longer body, not gating criteria

related.name: checklist
related.when: rows carry done/in-flight/planned state markers

related.name: verdict-grid
related.when: options scored against shared criteria

related.name: list
related.when: declared statements — the `principles` variant

related.name: list-tabular
related.when: rows carry structured metadata alongside the name and description

components.name: list-steps
components.description: Horizontal row of ordered step cards, each with a full description body (the `vertical` variant stacks them instead).
components.purpose: Use for richer sequential processes where each step needs a paragraph rather than a label. More verbose than timeline; more structured than a plain ordered list.

capacity.note: past five steps the row begins to crowd

steps.description: Ordered list; each li gets a step number. Body can be one paragraph or a nested bullet list.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: list-steps -->

## How to roll this out.

1. First step — a sentence describing what you do here.
2. Second step — a sentence describing what you do here.
3. Third step — a sentence describing what you do here.
4. Fourth step — a sentence describing what you do here.

whenToUse.title: Steps need a sentence each
whenToUse.body: When each step carries a label plus a sentence of description. Lighter rosters of steps with short labels use the `timeline` variant; richer descriptions belong on the default cards.

whenToUse.title: Three to five steps
whenToUse.body: Two steps wastes the layout's ledger feel; six begins to crowd. Group adjacent steps or split the process at a natural phase break.

whenToUse.title: Prefix word names the unit
whenToUse.body: The default `STEP` prefix can swap to `PHASE`, `STAGE`, `MILESTONE`, `RANK`, or `TIER`. Pick the noun that matches how the audience already thinks about the process.

antiPatterns.title: Light labels, no body
antiPatterns.body: If each step is a single label with no description, use the `timeline` variant (dots on a spine). The default step cards earn their chrome only when the body adds substance.

antiPatterns.title: Parallel options
antiPatterns.body: If the rows are alternatives the audience compares, use `cards-grid` or `verdict-grid`. The numbered prefix here reads as sequence — using it for parallel items mis-cues the audience.

antiPatterns.title: Author-typed step numbers
antiPatterns.body: Don't write `**STEP 01**` into the markdown. The badge is CSS-generated from the `ol` counter; manual numbering double-stamps and breaks on reordering.

related.name: list-criteria
related.when: gating requirements rather than a sequence of actions

related.name: split-panel
related.when: phase label + heading on the left, steps on the right

related.name: roadmap
related.when: phased grid across multiple workstreams

related.name: list
related.when: tenets or values (the `principles` variant) rather than a sequence

components.name: list-tabular
components.description: Hairline-ruled ledger of items — name on the left, body on the right.
components.purpose: Use for compact reference tables: glossary-style entries, key/value pairs, specs. Four primary variants (def, metric, spec, register) tune the visual treatment; secondary modifiers (rule, solid, stacked, outline) refine each.

rows.description: Each numbered item (`1.`) is one row — the name on the line, with an optional nested bullet for its description or value. The leading column is the auto counter.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: list-tabular -->

## Slide heading.

1. First entry
   - Description or value for the first entry.
2. Second entry
   - Description or value for the second entry.
3. Third entry
   - Description or value for the third entry.
4. Fourth entry
   - Description or value for the fourth entry.

whenToUse.title: Compact reference rows
whenToUse.body: Five or more rows where each row is a name plus a short description or value. Glossary-style entries, key/value pairs, technical specs.

whenToUse.title: Pick one primary variant
whenToUse.body: `def` for editorial, `metric` for tiled values, `spec` for technical keys, `register` for tagged pills. Default (no variant) is the hairline ledger.

whenToUse.title: Numbered automatically
whenToUse.body: Author as `ol` (`1.` source). The leading column is the counter — `def` and `spec.stacked` enlarge it to span both rows.

antiPatterns.title: Three or fewer rows
antiPatterns.body: The ledger needs density to justify its shape. For two to four items, reach for cards-stack — the rows get the room to breathe.

antiPatterns.title: Long per-row prose
antiPatterns.body: Each row is a name plus a sentence. If the description runs two or three sentences, move to cards-stack or split across slides.

antiPatterns.title: Stacking two primary variants
antiPatterns.body: `def`, `metric`, `spec`, and `register` are mutually exclusive. Pair each only with its secondary modifier (def+rule, metric+solid, spec+stacked, register+outline).

related.name: glossary
related.when: term/definition pairs with auto-derived range pill

related.name: cards-stack
related.when: two or three richer items, not a ledger

related.name: actors
related.when: the left column is a named person, not a key

related.name: list
related.when: rows are bullets without a label-plus-description shape

components.name: logo-wall
components.description: A grid of customer, partner, or funder logos as social proof.
components.purpose: Use for the credibility slide — the 'trusted by' / 'our funders' / 'participating agencies' wall. Marks render desaturated and uniform so mismatched brand colours don't fight each other; the `color` variant keeps them branded when the hues carry meaning.

eyebrow.description: Optional kicker above the headline — wrap a short label in backticks, e.g. `Trusted by`.

title.description: Optional headline above the wall. A claim earns its place (‘400+ teams run board prep on Lattice’); a bare label (‘Customers’) does not.

logos.description: One list item per mark, authored as `- ![Brand name](brand.svg)`. The alt text is the accessible label, not a rendered caption. SVG is preferred so marks stay crisp at projector scale.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: logo-wall -->

`Trusted by`

## The headline claim the logos back up.

- ![First brand](logo-1.svg)
- ![Second brand](logo-2.svg)
- ![Third brand](logo-3.svg)
- ![Fourth brand](logo-4.svg)
- ![Fifth brand](logo-5.svg)
- ![Sixth brand](logo-6.svg)

whenToUse.title: The proof is the logos
whenToUse.body: Customers, partners, funders, accreditations, participating agencies — anywhere a set of recognisable marks carries more weight than a sentence. The audience scans the wall and concludes 'serious company keeps this company.'

whenToUse.title: Marks read as one texture
whenToUse.body: The default desaturates every mark to the same restrained grey so a loud red logo doesn't outshout a quiet one. The wall reads as a single credential, not a ransom note of competing brand colours.

whenToUse.title: Eight to eighteen marks
whenToUse.body: Enough to signal breadth, few enough that each is legible. Fewer than six looks thin; past eighteen the marks shrink below recognition — curate to the most recognisable names or split across two slides.

antiPatterns.title: Names that need a sentence
antiPatterns.body: If each entity needs a role, a quote, or a metric beside it, this is the wrong layout. Use `actors` (who owns what), `cards-grid` (a short body per item), or `quote` (a single testimonial).

antiPatterns.title: Logos nobody recognises
antiPatterns.body: A wall of unknown marks proves nothing and asks the audience to squint. If the names don't carry on sight, state the count as a `big-number` ('400+ teams') instead.

antiPatterns.title: Mismatched raster art
antiPatterns.body: Low-resolution PNG logos pulled from web pages look ragged at projector scale and break the uniform grey treatment. Source vector (SVG) marks; the desaturation assumes clean edges.

related.name: actors
related.when: each named entity owns a responsibility, not just lends its logo

related.name: cards-grid
related.when: each item needs a line of body text, not just a mark

related.name: big-number
related.when: the proof is a count ('400+ teams'), not the individual marks

related.name: quote
related.when: one customer's testimonial carries the slide

related.name: image
related.when: a single visual, not a grid of marks, is the evidence

components.name: map
components.description: A world-countries (or US-states) basemap that fills regions by value (choropleth) or category (highlight) so the audience leaves knowing where.
components.purpose: Use when the story is geographic — program reach, service territories, where the grants landed, the regions you operate in. Author a value per named region (full name, postal/ISO code, or a common alias); choropleth shades each region on a single-hue ramp (low→high), while `highlight` gives each named region its own categorical colour. The default basemap is the **world** (Equal Earth projection); add `us` (or `usa`) for the US-states map. On the world map you can also name a continent, a bloc (`European Union`, `ASEAN`), or a stated category (`Global South`, `Global North`, `Global South — Africa`) and the kernel fills every member. Because the term is contested, two sourced views of the Global South ship: `Global South` (G77 + China) and `Global South — Brandt Line` (the 1980 North–South divide) — pick the framing your deck argues. Regions the basemap can't match are reported in the legend, never silently dropped.

title.description: Slide heading — name the geography and the takeaway (‘Where the program runs’).

regions.description: One li per region (or group). Lead with the name — world (default): full (`Brazil`), ISO (`BR`), alias (`Burma`), or a group (`European Union`, `Sub-Saharan Africa`, `Global South`) that expands to its members; US (`map us`): full (`California`), postal (`CA`), or abbreviation (`Calif.`) — then a trailing inline-code value: `Brazil \`4.2\``. In choropleth the value drives the ramp; in highlight it's an optional legend label. Names the basemap can't resolve surface as muted ‘?’ legend rows.

components.skeleton:
<!-- _class: map -->

## Where the program runs.

- Kenya `4.2`
- Nigeria `3.1`
- India `2.8`
- Brazil `2.2`

whenToUse.title: The story is geographic
whenToUse.body: Reach for a map only when WHERE is the point — coverage areas, jurisdictions, service territories, where the grants or pilots landed. If the geography is incidental and the comparison is really between named things, a `progress` or `stats` row reads faster than a basemap.

whenToUse.title: Choropleth for magnitude, highlight for membership
whenToUse.body: Use the default choropleth when each region carries a number and the audience needs the gradient — how much, where. Use `highlight` when the named regions are a set (the eight pilot states, the four regions we serve) and there is no magnitude to rank.

whenToUse.title: Name regions any common way
whenToUse.body: Country names resolve by full name, ISO code, or common alias (`Brazil` / `BR` / `Burma`→Myanmar); on `map us`, by full name, postal code, or abbreviation (`California` / `CA` / `Calif.`). A name the basemap can't bind is reported as a muted ‘?’ row in the legend and stamped on the figure — fix the spelling, don't ignore the gap.

antiPatterns.title: A map as decoration
antiPatterns.body: If the regions aren't the comparison — you just want a US-shaped graphic behind some numbers — drop the basemap. An `image` scrim or a `stats` row carries headline figures without implying the geography is the message.

antiPatterns.title: Too many shades to read
antiPatterns.body: A choropleth past a dozen distinct values asks the eye to rank colours it can't separate. Bucket the values, switch to `highlight` for a categorical read, or lead with a `progress` ranking and keep the map as support.

antiPatterns.title: Sub-region precision the basemap doesn't have
antiPatterns.body: The basemaps draw US states and world countries — not counties, districts, sub-national regions, or city pins, and the world cut (110m) omits the smallest city-states. If the story lives below that line, a labelled `image` of the real map serves better than forcing it onto the basemap.

related.name: progress
related.when: the regions are really a ranking — labelled bars compare magnitudes faster than shades

related.name: stats
related.when: a few headline figures with no geography to place them on

related.name: piechart
related.when: regional shares of a single whole rather than a value per place

related.name: image
related.when: the geography needs detail (counties, cities, routes) the basemap can't draw

components.name: math
components.description: Boardroom-quality math layouts for mathematicians, quants, ML researchers, physicists, statisticians, and economists. KaTeX-rendered equations with persona-appropriate surround.
components.purpose: Use when the slide IS the equation. KaTeX renders `$$…$$` as centred display blocks and `$…$` inline.
